section.title: Be Strong and Courageous
section.sectionRationale: The book opens in the gap left by a death. Moses, the servant through whom God brought Israel out of Egypt and gave the law, is gone, and a new leader stands on the edge of the promised land with a task too large for any man. Into that silence the LORD speaks, commissioning Joshua, renewing the ancient promise of the land, and pressing home one command four times over: be strong and courageous. It is one tight movement, the divine charge that launches the whole conquest.
section.thesis: The land is God's gift and God's oath, not Joshua's achievement; so the courage Joshua is commanded to have rests not on his own strength but on the presence of the LORD who never leaves him and on the word of the LORD he is to meditate on day and night.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the commission after Moses' death, verses 1 to 2; the promise of land and unfailing presence, verses 3 to 5; and the fourfold call to strong, courageous obedience anchored in the Book of the Law, verses 6 to 9.

units.label: The commission after Moses' death
units.note: Moses My servant is dead; now arise

verses.text: Now after the death of His servant Moses, the LORD spoke to {{yehoshua|Joshua}} son of Nun, Moses’ assistant, saying,
“Moses My servant is dead. Now therefore arise, you and all these people, and cross over the Jordan into the land that I am giving to the children of Israel.

units.label: The promise of land and unfailing presence
units.note: I will never leave you nor forsake you

verses.text: I have given you every place where the sole of your foot will tread, just as I promised to Moses.
Your territory shall extend from the wilderness and Lebanon to the great River Euphrates, all the land of the Hittites, and west as far as the Great Sea.
No one shall stand against you all the days of your life. As I was with Moses, so will I be with you; I will never leave you nor forsake you.

units.label: The fourfold call to courage and obedience
units.note: be strong and courageous; meditate on the Law

verses.text: Be {{chazaq|strong}} and {{amats|courageous}}, for you shall give these people the {{nachalah|inheritance}} of the land that I swore to their fathers I would give them.

Above all, be strong and very courageous. Be careful to observe all the {{torah|law}} that My servant Moses commanded you. Do not turn from it to the right or to the left, so that you may prosper wherever you go.

This Book of the Law must not depart from your mouth; {{hagah|meditate}} on it day and night, so that you may be careful to do everything written in it. For then you will prosper and succeed in all you do.
Have I not commanded you to be strong and courageous? Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged, for the LORD your God is with you wherever you go.”

yehoshua.gloss: Joshua, meaning the LORD saves or the LORD is salvation. Moses renamed Hoshea son of Nun to Yehoshua, Numbers 13:16. It is the same name, in its later Greek form Iesous, that the angel gives to Mary's son, Matthew 1:21; the leader who brings Israel into the land bears the very name of the greater Savior who brings His people into eternal rest.

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, firm, resolute; to take hold and hold fast. Repeated with amats as a paired command, it is the charge sounded four times over Joshua. The strength called for is not raw self-confidence but a firm grip on God's promise and God's word in the face of an overwhelming task.

amats.gloss: To be courageous, bold, alert; to steel oneself. Paired with chazaq throughout the charge, it summons a courage grounded not in Joshua's ability but in the assurance that the LORD goes with him and will not fail him.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, possession, allotted heritage. The land is Israel's nachalah because it is a gift handed down by promise, sworn to the fathers, not seized by right. The word governs the whole book: chapters 13 to 21 divide this inheritance tribe by tribe, and the New Testament lifts the idea to the imperishable inheritance kept in heaven, 1 Peter 1:4.

torah.gloss: Law, instruction, teaching. The Book of the Law, the covenant word given through Moses, is to shape everything Joshua does. Success in Canaan is tied not to military genius but to careful, wholehearted obedience to God's revealed word, turning aside neither to the right nor the left.

hagah.gloss: To meditate, mutter, murmur; to ponder something by speaking it under one's breath. To meditate on the Law day and night is to keep God's word constantly on the lips and in the mind, the same picture that opens Psalm 1. It is not idle reflection but the steady, spoken rehearsing of God's word that produces obedience.

basic.context: Moses is dead, and Joshua, his long-time assistant, must lead a whole nation across the Jordan into a land full of fortified cities and strong peoples. The task is impossible on paper. So God speaks. He does not hand Joshua a battle plan; He gives him a promise and a command. The promise: I am giving you this land, and I will be with you as I was with Moses, and I will never leave you. The command, sounded four times, is the same: be strong and courageous. The key question the passage answers is: where does courage for an overwhelming calling actually come from?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that opens a book of conquest. The land promise here is specific to Israel and to Joshua's moment; we should not read every detail as a direct promise of real estate to us. Yet the New Testament deliberately takes God's words to Joshua, I will never leave you nor forsake you, and applies them to every believer, Hebrews 13:5. The abiding truth is that God commands courageous obedience and grounds it in His own faithful presence and His word, not in our strength.
basic.originalLanguages: The new leader is Yehoshua, the LORD saves, the very name later given to Jesus. The paired command chazaq and amats, be strong and be courageous, rings out four times. The land is Israel's nachalah, its inheritance by promise. And the secret to success is not the sword but the torah, God's word, which Joshua is to hagah, to meditate on, day and night.
basic.typology: Joshua stepping into Moses' place to lead God's people into the promised land foreshadows Christ, who takes us beyond what the law through Moses could do and leads His people into the true rest. The land itself, the inheritance, is a shadow of the eternal inheritance secured in Christ. The word that must never depart from Joshua's mouth points to Christ, the Word made flesh.
basic.christ: Joshua bears the name that Jesus would bear, the LORD saves. Where Joshua led one generation into a land they would eventually lose, Jesus leads all His people into a rest they can never lose, Hebrews 4:8-9. The command be strong and courageous, grounded in God's presence, finds its deepest yes in Christ, who is Immanuel, God with us, and who promises, I am with you always.
basic.god: God is the giver: the land is His to give, sworn by oath to the fathers, so the whole enterprise rests on His faithfulness, not Israel's merit. He is present: I will be with you, I will never leave you nor forsake you. And He is the God who speaks, binding His people's flourishing to obedience to His word. Courage is commanded because the God who commands it goes with the one He sends.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 31 records Moses charging Joshua with the same words before he dies. Psalm 1 pictures the blessed one who meditates on God's law day and night. Hebrews 13 lifts I will never leave you nor forsake you into a promise for every Christian. And Hebrews 4 shows that Joshua's rest was only a shadow of the rest we have in Christ.
basic.oneStory: The promise of land made to Abraham now moves toward fulfillment as a new leader named the LORD saves stands ready to bring Israel in. God's presence and word, not human strength, will win it. The whole scene points beyond Canaan to the greater Joshua, Jesus, who leads His people into the rest that lasts forever.

context.literary: Joshua opens the way many biblical books do, with a death and a succession: after the death of Moses. The passage is a commissioning speech, almost entirely God's direct words to Joshua, framed by the crossing of the Jordan that will dominate the next chapters. The fourfold be strong and courageous, verses 6, 7, 9, and later 18, is the drumbeat of the section, and the command to meditate on the Book of the Law ties Joshua tightly to Deuteronomy, the book of Moses just completed.
context.historical: Joshua leads Israel around the thirteenth century BC, at the close of the wilderness years, poised on the plains of Moab to cross the flooding Jordan into Canaan. Canaan was a patchwork of fortified city-states with trained armies and, to Israel's eyes, giants in the land, Numbers 13. A leadership transition at such a moment was perilous; Israel had already seen a generation die in the wilderness for failing to trust God at the border. The described territory, from the wilderness to Lebanon, the Euphrates, and the Great Sea, sketches the ideal extent of the promise sworn to Abraham, Genesis 15:18.
context.canonical: Joshua stands at the head of the Former Prophets and shows the beginning of the fulfillment of the land promise given to Abraham and renewed through Moses. It self-consciously builds on Deuteronomy, quoting the charge to meditate on the Law. Its command about God's abiding presence is picked up directly in the New Testament, and Joshua himself, and the rest he brings, becomes a key type that Hebrews uses to point to the greater rest in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To narrate God's commissioning of Joshua after Moses' death, to renew the promise of the land as God's sworn gift, and to press home that Israel's leader, and by extension Israel, must find courage in the LORD's presence and unwavering obedience to His word, not in military strength.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The land grant and the specific commission belong to Joshua's unique moment in redemptive history and are not a promise of territory or guaranteed worldly success to readers today. What is prescriptive, confirmed by the New Testament's own use of the passage, is the call to courageous obedience grounded in God's presence and word, and the assurance that God will never leave nor forsake His people.

debates.question: Is the promise you will prosper and succeed a guarantee of worldly success for believers who read the Bible?

views.label: A covenant promise tied to obedience, not a prosperity formula
views.case: The prospering promised to Joshua is explicitly bound to careful obedience to the whole Law and to the covenant task God gave him; it is God's faithfulness to His own promise about the land, not a general pledge that Bible reading yields wealth or ease. Read in context, and alongside the suffering of faithful people throughout Scripture, it cannot be turned into a success formula.

views.label: A principle that God blesses obedience, rightly qualified
views.case: There is a real and abiding principle that God honors those who treasure and obey His word, and that a life shaped by Scripture is a flourishing life in the deepest sense. The danger is only in flattening that into guaranteed material prosperity; understood as the true flourishing of covenant faithfulness, the promise still speaks.

debates.bearing: Both guard against a prosperity-gospel misreading: the text ties blessing to wholehearted obedience within God's redemptive purpose, and Scripture's own witness shows that faithfulness often costs, even as God remains present and good.

study.originalLanguages: Everything in the charge points away from Joshua's own resources to God. The leader's name is Yehoshua, the LORD saves, a quiet sign that salvation is God's doing, not the commander's. The land is not a prize to be won but a nachalah, an inheritance, sworn by oath to the fathers and simply given: I have given you every place. Four times the paired command lands, chazaq and amats, be strong and be courageous, and each time it is anchored not in a pep talk about ability but in the presence of God, for the LORD your God is with you wherever you go. The engine of obedience is the torah, the Book of the Law, which must not depart from Joshua's mouth as he learns to hagah, to meditate, to mutter God's word to himself day and night, the same image that opens Psalm 1. The vocabulary preaches the point: the conquest will be won by a God who saves, keeps His oath, stays present, and speaks, through a leader whose strength is to hold fast to that word.

typology.type: Joshua, the LORD saves, succeeding Moses to lead Israel into the land
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, whose name means the LORD saves, leading His people beyond what the law could do into the true and lasting rest, Hebrews 4:8-10.

typology.type: The promised land as Israel's inheritance
typology.fulfillment: The imperishable inheritance kept in heaven for those who are in Christ, 1 Peter 1:3-4 and Hebrews 9:15.

typology.type: The word that must never depart from Joshua's mouth
typology.fulfillment: Christ the living Word, and the indwelling of His word in His people by the Spirit, John 1:14 and Colossians 3:16.

study.christ: The very name Joshua, Yehoshua, is the name Jesus, the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21. The parallel is not accidental: as Joshua leads Israel out of the wilderness and into the land God promised, so Jesus leads His people out of sin and death into the inheritance God has promised. Yet the writer to the Hebrews is careful to show the greater glory of the second Joshua: if the first Joshua had given them true rest, God would not have spoken later of another rest; so the rest that remains for the people of God is found not in Canaan but in Christ, Hebrews 4:8-9. The command be strong and courageous, grounded in I will never leave you nor forsake you, reaches its fullness in Immanuel, God with us, who says to His own, I am with you always, to the very end of the age, Matthew 28:20.
study.god: This opening scene is God-centered from the first word. The land is His to give, sworn by His own oath to the fathers, so Israel receives it as grace, not as wages. He is the God of unfailing presence: as I was with Moses, so will I be with you; I will never leave you nor forsake you. He is the God who speaks and binds His people's flourishing to obedient attention to His word. And He is the God who commands courage precisely because He supplies the ground for it, His own abiding presence. Joshua is charged to be strong, but every reason given is something about God: God gives, God swore, God is with you. The leader's task is not to generate courage but to rest his courage on the character of the LORD.

crossReferences.note: Moses charges Joshua and Israel with the same words, be strong and courageous, and I will never leave you nor forsake you, just before his death.
The blessed one meditates on the law of the LORD day and night and prospers, the very pattern commanded to Joshua.
God's promise to Joshua, I will never leave you nor forsake you, is applied directly to every believer as ground for contentment and courage.
Joshua's rest in the land was only a shadow; a greater Sabbath rest remains for the people of God in Christ.
The risen Jesus, all authority given to Him, commissions and promises His presence, I am with you always, the fuller echo of the charge to Joshua.

study.oneStory: The promise of a land, first sworn to Abraham and carried through Moses, now stands on the threshold of fulfillment as a leader named the LORD saves prepares to bring Israel across the Jordan. The scene insists that the inheritance is God's gift, won by His presence and His word rather than human might. And it looks past Canaan itself: the first Joshua brings one generation into a land they will one day lose, but he points to the greater Joshua, Jesus, who leads all His people into the rest that can never be lost.

quiz.q: What command does God repeat to Joshua throughout this passage?

options.text: Be strong and courageous, grounded in God's presence and word.
options.note: The charge sounds four times and is anchored in the LORD being with him wherever he goes.

options.text: Prepare your weapons and train the army for battle.
options.note: Israel will fight, but God's actual repeated command is about courage and obedience, not military preparation.

options.text: Choose capable officers to share the leadership burden.
options.note: Delegation appears elsewhere in Moses' story; here God's repeated word is be strong and courageous.

options.text: Wait quietly and do nothing until the enemy attacks.
options.note: God commands Joshua to arise and cross over, the opposite of passivity.

quiz.q: On what does God ground the courage He commands?

options.text: On His own unfailing presence: I will never leave you nor forsake you.
options.note: Verse 5 and verse 9 root the courage in God's presence, not Joshua's ability.

options.text: On the promise that Joshua will meditate on the Law and obey it.
options.note: Obedience to the word is central, but the deepest ground given for courage is God's presence with him.

options.text: On Joshua's proven skill as Moses' assistant.
options.note: Joshua's experience is real, but the text grounds courage in God, not in Joshua's resume.

options.text: On the weakness of the peoples living in Canaan.
options.note: Canaan's peoples were strong and fortified; the ground of courage is God's presence, not their weakness.

quiz.q: How is Joshua to relate to the Book of the Law?

options.text: It must not depart from his mouth; he is to meditate on it day and night and carefully obey it.
options.note: Verse 8 ties his success to constant meditation on and obedience to God's word.

options.text: He should read it once before crossing the Jordan.
options.note: He is to know it, but the command is continual, day and night, not a single reading.

options.text: He should keep it stored safely in the ark and consult the priests.
options.note: The Law was kept by the ark, but Joshua is personally to meditate on it constantly.

options.text: He may set it aside now that Moses is gone.
options.note: The opposite: the word Moses gave is to govern everything Joshua does.

quiz.q: Why is the land called Israel's inheritance rather than something they earned?

options.text: Because God is giving it as a gift, sworn by oath to their fathers.
options.note: Verses 2 to 6 present the land as God's sworn gift, an inheritance, not a wage.

options.text: Because Israel's obedience in the wilderness had earned it.
options.note: Obedience matters, but the land is grounded in God's oath, not Israel's merit; that generation had actually failed.

options.text: Because they were the strongest nation in the region.
options.note: Israel was not the strongest; the land is a gift by promise, not a spoil of superior power.

options.text: Because they had purchased it from the Canaanites.
options.note: There is no purchase; the land is God's to give and He gives it by oath.

quiz.q: How does Joshua point forward to Jesus?

options.text: He bears the name the LORD saves and leads God's people into the inheritance, foreshadowing Jesus who leads His people into lasting rest.
options.note: Joshua and Jesus share a name and a role; Hebrews 4 shows Joshua's rest was a shadow of Christ's.

options.text: He shows that a strong human leader is all God's people really need.
options.note: Joshua leads well, but the passage roots everything in God; the greater Joshua is Christ, not merely a strong man.

options.text: He proves that the promised land is the final goal of salvation.
options.note: Canaan is a shadow; the true inheritance and rest are found in Christ, not in the land itself.

options.text: He has no real connection to Jesus.
options.note: The shared name and the pattern of leading God's people into rest clearly point to Christ.

questions: God commanded courage but grounded it in His presence, not in Joshua's ability. Where are you being called to be strong and courageous, and how would it change things to lean on God's presence rather than your own resources?
Joshua was to meditate on God's word day and night. What would it look like for Scripture to be that constantly on your lips and mind this week?
The land was a gift, an inheritance sworn by God, not something Israel earned. How does receiving your standing with God as gift rather than achievement free you to obey Him?
God's promise to Joshua, I will never leave you nor forsake you, is spoken to every believer in Hebrews 13. How does that promise steady you in the task or fear you are facing right now?

Joshua bore the name the LORD saves and led one generation into a land they would lose; Jesus leads His people into a rest they can never lose. How does knowing the greater Joshua change the way you face your calling?

translationNotes.note: The BSB uses em dashes to set off all the land of the Hittites; these have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording. The Great Sea is the Mediterranean, as the BSB footnote notes.
The promise I will never leave you nor forsake you is marked in the BSB as cited in Hebrews 13:5, where it is applied to all believers.
Joshua son of Nun is Yehoshua, the LORD saves; Moses had renamed him from Hoshea, Numbers 13:16. The name is the same one later given to Jesus.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 1:1-9. Prose commissioning speech. The em dashes in verse 4 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
